title: Repair After Yelling at Your Kid
dek: You’ve just screamed at your child. The sound stings the air. Your throat raw, chest heaving, your hands trembling, jaw clenched. You’re already planning the apology that will make this vanish. But repair isn’t a script. It’s a physical, slow-burning thing that starts where shame wants to sprint. Let’s begin in the body, where the truth lives.
bodyMarkdown:
What do you actually feel in your stomach right after you’ve yelled at your kid? Not the story you tell yourself about why it happened, not the self-recrimination that floods in, but the raw physical sensation. Most parents avoid this question. They bolt straight from shouting to smoothing things over, from the hot rush of anger to the cold scramble of control. That gap—the seconds when your body is still a live wire—is where repair actually begins. You just can’t stand to be in it. So you fill it with a rushed apology, a hug your child doesn’t want, a plea for reassurance that you’re not a monster. This isn’t repair. This is you asking your kid to soothe you. And they feel the difference in their bones.

## The Apology You’re About to Deliver Is a Form of Control

Your body is still humming with adrenaline. Your heart hasn’t slowed. Yet the words are already forming: “Mommy’s sorry, I didn’t mean that, you know I love you, right?” You’re not attuning to the small person in front of you. You’re stamping out the fire you started because the smoke of it burns your own lungs. A real repair acknowledges the rupture without demanding the child end it. Harriet Lerner, a psychologist who has spent decades studying apologies, writes that a true apology must be delivered without the expectation that it will be accepted and without the word “but” sneaking in to excuse your behavior. Rushing in with “I’m sorry you made me so angry” or “I was just tired” is not an apology; it’s an argument for your own righteousness disguised as remorse. The child becomes an audience for your self-justification. They learn that when you hurt them, their role is to absorb the hurt and then immediately reassure you that you’re still a good parent. This teaches them to ignore their own body’s signals—the tight chest, the churning stomach—in order to take care of yours. The apology you were about to blurt is a transactional trick, and it builds a relationship on unspoken lies.

## Your Nervous System Doesn’t Care About Your Good Intentions

You cannot repair while your own nervous system is in fight-or-flight. This isn’t a metaphor. The sympathetic nervous system drives the yelling: cortisol and adrenaline surge, your heart rate spikes above 100 bpm, blood diverts from your prefrontal cortex to your limbs. You are biologically incapable of attunement in this state. Your child’s nervous system mirrors yours—so their body is also flooded with stress hormones, freezing or fleeing internally even if they stay put. If you walk toward them with a trembling voice and wild eyes, insisting on a hug, you are two dysregulated bodies colliding. There is no repair here, only further overstimulation. Soraya Chemaly, in her work on anger, points out that anger is not a pathology but a signal, yet the way we express it often bypasses the signal and goes straight to harm. The signal might have been legitimate: boundary violation, overwhelm, exhaustion. But the repair must first address the harm, and that requires a regulated nervous system. Before you say a word, you need to feel your own feet on the floor. Put your hand on your sternum and press until you feel the bone. Breathe in through your nose for a count of four, hold for four, exhale through your mouth for six. Do it again. You’re not stalling to withhold love; you’re giving your neurobiology a chance to shift so the love you offer isn’t drenched in panic. A few drops of a plant ally like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) can calm the shaking hands and quiet the inner alarm enough to think clearly, but the real work is waiting long enough that your presence stops feeling like a threat.

## The Body Remembers What Words Try to Erase

You can feel the heat still in your throat, a raw scrape from the volume you unleashed. Maybe your jaw aches from clenching, or your shoulders are up around your ears. These sensations matter because your child’s body recorded the event with equal precision. A yelled-at child experiences a spike in cortisol that can linger for twenty minutes or more. Their shoulders might have risen to protect their neck. Their stomach might be tight, the way a prey animal’s body prepares to run. They may not have words for any of this, but the body remembers. When you rush in with a verbal apology, you’re skipping over the physical residue of fear. Gabor Maté’s work on the mind-body connection, especially in his book [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20), shows how unexpressed emotional experience and chronic stress embed themselves in the body’s tissues, setting the stage for illness. While you’re not giving your child a disease in one bad afternoon, you are teaching them to dissociate from bodily sensation—to smile when the gut says danger. A repair that ignores the body is a repair that never fully lands. You might notice your child’s hands are still in fists, or their eyes won’t meet yours. These are not signs of defiance; they are signs of a nervous system still on alert. Acknowledge this out loud: “Your body might still feel tight from when I yelled. I see that. We can just be here a minute.” This naming of the physical experience is more soothing than any generic “I’m sorry” because it shows you see them, not just your own story.

## A Five-Minute Repair That Has Nothing to Do With Talking

So you’ve calmed your own breathing. Your heart rate has dropped. The ringing silence in the room is almost unbearable, but you don’t fill it. Here is a practice you can do in under five minutes that begins repair without a single word. First, get close to ground level with your child. If they’re on the floor, sit on the floor. Not hovering, not reaching. Just sit, leaving a gap of a few feet. Place your palms face-up on your thighs. This open-handed posture signals non-aggression to the mammalian brain. Now, simply breathe audibly, slow and steady, so your child can hear the rhythm. Let your eyes rest on their body, not their face—look at their hands, their feet, the slope of their small back. This removes the pressure of eye contact. If they move closer, let them. If they don’t, you can quietly say, “I’m here now.” Not “I’m sorry,” not “Look at me.” Just presence. After a few minutes, you might notice the child’s breathing syncing with yours, the way bodies do in co-regulation. The rupture isn’t fixed, but the nervous system repair has begun. The apology, if it comes later—and it should—will have a foundation beneath it. If the day is wrecked anyway and bedtime is approaching, a cup of [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can signal to both your bodies that the crisis has passed, a warm ritual that marks the end of the storm without forcing a conversation the child isn’t ready for.

## Why “I’m Sorry” Makes It Worse (And What To Say Instead)

An unqualified “I’m sorry” from a parent who just yelled lands like a command: “Let’s move on.” It asks the child to erase the event without any understanding of what just happened to them. Lerner emphasizes that a meaningful apology must include a specific acknowledgment of the harm and a promise not to repeat it, but with yelling, the promise is tricky. You will yell again. You know you will. So don’t make false pledges. Instead, say something that names the impact and your own lack of control without making the child responsible for your emotional regulation. For example: “I just said really loud, scary words to you. I lost control of my anger, and I see how your face went still. That was not your fault. I’m working on this, and I know yelling hurts you.” There is no “but” in there. There is no excuse. The child hears that their perception of being hurt was accurate, that you saw the effect in real time. If you were angry about a shared issue—a boundary crossed, a toy thrown—you can return to that later, calmly. The repair has to come first, or the discipline never registers. Chemaly reminds us that anger is a tool for recognizing injustice but it becomes destructive when it eclipses the person we’re angry at. In that moment, your child was not a person; they were a target. The repair is about restoring their personhood. The words you use need to make clear that you see them as a separate being with a body that can be frightened and a heart that can ache.

## The Soul of the Yeller (Thomas Moore’s Dark Gift)

Here’s a counterintuitive claim: yelling at your child is not a sign that you’re a failed parent. It’s a sign that something in your own inner life is begging to be heard, and it has been so thoroughly silenced that it can only come out sideways. Thomas Moore, in his care of the soul philosophy, argues that what we call psychological problems are often the soul’s distorted attempts to emerge. Your rage, your loss of control, is a neglected part of yourself thrashing in the dark. This does not excuse the harm you caused. Your child does not deserve to carry the weight of your unexpressed grief, your exhaustion, your old wounds. But if you treat your yelling only as a pathology to be eliminated, you will miss the message it carries. What were you feeling in the hour before you screamed? Probably not serenity. Probably a slow buildup of resentment, physical tiredness, a hundred ignored micro-frustrations. The anger was a flare fired from a deeper self that has not been allowed to speak its needs. Repair with your child might require you to first listen to that self, without defensiveness. Journal about the rage later that evening. Ask it what it wants. You may find it wants rest, or recognition, or the grief of a loss you haven’t named. When you can hold your own darkness without shaming it, you are less likely to fling it at your child. This is the deeper repair that transforms rupture into a doorway for your own growth. It won’t stop you from ever yelling again, but it will change what you do with the aftermath.

## FAQ

**Should I apologize if I was right to be angry?**  
Yes, because the anger itself was not the harm; the yelling was. You can be legitimately furious about a child running into the street or hitting a sibling. The feeling is valid. The frightened, screaming voice is what cut through their nervous system. You can say, “I was very scared you would get hurt, and I yelled. I wish I had grabbed your hand without shouting. My scared came out as loud, and that wasn’t safe for you.” This separates the emotion from the expression, teaching your child that feelings are welcome even when behaviors need repair.

**What if my kid won’t talk to me after I yelled?**
